name: Ohio
tooltip: Ohio — Cat 3: Strong publicity statute (Ch. 2741). 60-year post-mortem. No AI-specific overlay yet.
rightOfPublicity: Yes — Ohio Rev. Code Ch. 2741, specifically § 2741.02. Broad "persona" definition covering name, voice, signature, photograph, image, likeness, AND distinctive appearance, gestures, and mannerisms. Living and deceased persons.
postMortem: Yes — 60 years after death. Ohio Rev. Code § 2741.02. (10-year post-mortem for deceased Ohio National Guard or U.S. armed forces members.)
aiCoverage: No AI-specific statute. The broad "persona" definition could be argued to reach AI-generated content under existing language, but no Ohio court has tested it.
remedy: Civil suit under Ohio Rev. Code § 2741.06 for actual damages plus statutory damages of $2,500 to $10,000, profits, attorney's fees, and injunctive relief.
whatsInteresting: Ohio's "distinctive appearance, gestures, and mannerisms" language is unusually broad — broader than most states — and could be argued to reach AI-generated deepfakes even without an AI-specific statute. The state hasn't passed AI-likeness legislation yet, but the existing publicity statute is strong enough that you have real leverage. The $2,500-$10,000 statutory damages range is also better than most Category 3 states.
primarySources: Ohio Rev. Code § 2741.02 (persona definition and post-mortem)
